0004_print_260112225745_generation_minim.content:
# Philosophical Conversation

**Topic:** Is war moral and ethical, can it ever be justifiable

**Participants:** Marcus Aurelius, Arthur Schopenhauer, Yukio Mishima

**Date:** 2026-01-12 22:57:45

---

## System

Is war moral and ethical, can it ever be justifiable
Participants: Marcus Aurelius, Arthur Schopenhauer, Yukio Mishima

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, when it comes to pass, is not something I would seek or celebrate, but a burden ordained by necessity to defend the common good and the weak from aggression. If it is fought in accordance with justice and temperance—to restore order, protect the innocent, and uphold the law of the good—not for頃 or glory, it may be justified as a last resort. Yet beware the ruinous passions that feed it: pride, vengeance, and the craving for conquest corrupt the soul and make victory hollow. The true aim must be virtue—training the will toward endurance, self-control, and fidelity to duty—so that even victory leaves the victor wiser and more humane. If misused for domination or fear, it forfeits its legitimacy and becomes a stain on the character of both rulers and citizens.

---

## Arthur Schopenhauer

Schopenhauer: War is not a moral triumph but a brutal eruption of the blind will to life, gnawing away at the very suffering it pretends to defend. There is no true ethical justification for war, only different degrees of human misery inflicted by rulers and factions upon the masses. If one must speak of legitimacy, it is paradoxically the defense of the innocent and the restraint of worse violence, a grudging last resort rather than a noble ideal. The only lasting ethic I recognize is compassion and pity, which ought to guide us toward negation of the will and avoidance of needless suffering, not the glamour of conquest.

---

## Yukio Mishima

As a samurai of the modern world, I believe war can be justified only when it is a solemn act to defend the venerable soul of a nation against decay and dissolution, not for vanity or conquest. True legitimacy hinges on duty fulfilled with restraint, courage, and an unwavering commitment to honor, even in the face of inevitable suffering. Like Aurelius, I acknowledge the danger of pride and tyranny that corrupts both victor and vanquished, yet I hold that the test of a people is whether they defend their sacred values with disciplined resolve. Unlike Schopenhauer, I do not see compassion as an absolute negation of the Will; I see beauty and integrity as inseparable from action taken to preserve a living tradition. In the end, war is a tragedy that reveals or destroys a nation's spirit, and its morality rests on the purity of motive and the fidelity of those who stand to defend what is most precious.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, when it comes to pass, is a burden ordained by necessity to defend the innocent and the common good against aggression. If fought in accordance with justice and temperance, not for glory or domination, it may be justified as a last resort and a duty to preserve order and the law of virtue. Yet the passions—pride, vengeance, appetite for conquest—must be kept under control, for they corrupt both victor and vanquished. The true aim should be the restoration of rightful order, the protection of the weak, and the fortification of a calm, courageous spirit. If misused to appease anger or to tyrannize others, it forfeits its legitimacy and wounds the soul of the nation.

---

## Arthur Schopenhauer

War is not a triumph of ethics but the brutal eruption of the will to life, a spectacle of suffering that reveals the deeper misery of existence. There is no true moral justification for it; any “justification” stands only as a grudging calculus of greater or lesser harm, never as a noble ideal. If pressed to acknowledge a possibility, one might say defense of the innocent and restraint of worse violence can render it a last resort, yet such ends are always compromised by illusion and harm. The only lasting ethic I recognize is compassion and the negation of the will, which would counsel avoidance of war whenever possible.

---

## Yukio Mishima

As a samurai of the modern age, I concede that true legitimacy in war rests on defending a nation's sacred values and its living spirit, not on vanity or conquest. The test is discipline: are we acting with duty, restraint, and unwavering resolve to preserve what is noble, even at great cost? Yet I agree with Aurelius that pride and tyranny corrupt both victor and vanquished, and with Schopenhauer that war reveals human misery rather than moral triumph. Still, I would argue that when a people faces dissolution, a righteous, measured defense—carried out with honor and fidelity to tradition—can be a necessary catalyst for renewing virtue. In this light, war becomes tragic but not devoid of moral meaning, a crucible through which a nation's soul is tested.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, if it comes, must be because it is necessary to defend the innocent and uphold rightful order, not for vanity or conquest. It should be undertaken with justice, temperance, and the discipline of the commander so that victory leaves the victor wiser and more humane. The passions—pride, vengeance, the craving for glory—must be restrained, lest they corrupt both the aggressor and the subjected. If misused as an instrument of tyranny, it forfeits legitimacy and harms the soul of the state as well as its people. Let the aim always be restoration of virtue and the protection of the weak, with a clear preference for peace whenever the just end can be achieved without bloodshed.

---

## Arthur Schopenhauer

War, in my view, is not a moral triumph but the eruption of the blind will to life, showing the deepest misery of existence rather than its nobility. Any so-called justification is merely a pragmatic calculus of greater or lesser harm, never a noble ideal. If pressed to acknowledge a possibility, one might concede defense of the innocent and restraint of worse violence as a grudging last resort, yet such ends are always compromised by suffering and illusion. The only enduring ethic I recognize is compassion and the negation of the will, which counsels avoidance of war whenever possible. Thus, while war may be unavoidable at times, its morality remains a grim paradox, never a true justification.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, if it must come, is a burden laid upon us by necessity to defend the innocent and uphold just order. It may be justifiable only when undertaken with temperance, discipline, and a purpose to restore harmony, not for glory or vengeance. The true aim must be the protection of the weak and the establishment of rightful rule, so that the soul remains steadfast and the republic uncorrupted. Let us beware the passions—pride, hatred, thirst for conquest—that spoil even righteous acts and wound the victor as surely as the vanquished. In all, war is a grim last resort, to be pursued only to preserve virtue and peace.

---

## Arthur Schopenhauer

War is not a triumph of ethics but the violent eruption of the blind will to life, revealing the deeper misery of existence rather than nobility. Any so-called justification is merely a grim calculus of greater or lesser harm, never a true noble ideal, and the defense of the innocent remains a grudging exception rather than a justification for war as such. The only enduring ethic I recognize is compassion and the negation of the will, which counsels avoidance of war whenever possible. Therefore, when it occurs, it is a tragic necessity rather than a moral achievement, and its legitimacy rests on minimizing suffering and never awakening the vicious passions that feed further violence.

---

## Yukio Mishima

As a modern samurai, I concede that war may be justified only when it defends the sacred spirit and living tradition of a nation against dissolution and decay, not for vanity or conquest. The true test is discipline: are we fighting with duty, restraint, and fidelity to our code, even when cost mounts? I reject the sentimental glamour of war; its morality lies in the preservation of honor and the courage to endure, not in triumphalism. Yet I also bow before the truth that war unveils a people’s inner strength and its willingness to sacrifice for higher ends, a crucible that can renew a nation's soul if guided by a steadfast will toward virtue. In that sense, war remains tragedy rather than virtue, and its legitimacy rests on the nobility of the cause and the purity of motive.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, if it must come, is a burden laid upon us by necessity to defend the innocent and uphold rightful order. It may be justifiable as a last resort when it serves justice and protects the weak, ever tempered by temperance and the discipline of the soul. Yet we must beware the passions—pride, vengeance, the lust for conquest—that corrupt both victor and vanquished and hollow out the victory. The true aim is restoration of virtue and peace, so that the republic remains a place where virtue can flourish. If misused to tyrannize or to indulge cruelty, war ceases to be just and becomes a stain on the character of rulers and citizens alike.

---

## Arthur Schopenhauer

War is not a triumph of ethics but the violent eruption of the blind will to life, exposing the profundity of human misery rather than nobility. Any so-called justification is only a grim calculus of greater or lesser harm, never a true moral ideal. If one must concede a possibility, it is merely a reluctant defense of the innocent and a restraint upon worse violence, not an endorsement of war as such. The enduring ethic I recognize is compassion and the negation of the will, which counsels avoiding war whenever possible. Therefore, when war appears, it is a tragic necessity to be minimized, not a genuine moral achievement to be celebrated.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, if it must occur, is a grave burden laid upon us by necessity to defend the innocent and uphold rightful order. It may be justifiable only as a last resort, when all peaceable means have failed and the aim is to restore justice, not to indulge pride or conquest. Above all, the victor must keep the soul steady, tempering anger with discipline so that victory dignifies virtue rather than corrupts it. Let us beware the passions—vanity, vengeance, and the hunger for glory—that hollow any triumph. If war becomes a door to tyranny or cruelty, its very legitimacy is tarnished and the soul of the state suffers.

---

## Arthur Schopenhauer

War is not a triumph of ethics but the violent eruption of the blind will to life, exposing the deepest misery of existence rather than noble virtue. Any supposed justification is but a grim calculus of greater or lesser harm, never a true moral ideal. If pressed to allow a possibility, one might concede a grudging defense of the innocent and a restraint upon worse violence, yet such ends remain profoundly compromised by suffering. The enduring ethic I recognize is compassion and the negation of the will, which counsels avoidance of war whenever possible. When war must occur, it is a tragic necessity to be minimized, not a genuine moral achievement to be celebrated.

---

## Yukio Mishima

As a modern samurai, I hold that war is not a source of glory but a crucible that tests the nation's deepest commitments to tradition and essence. When a people faces dissolution, a disciplined, restrained defense—fought to preserve virtue, honor, and the living spirit—can be a just necessity, not conquest. Yet the morality of war demands purity of motive, temperance in action, and vigilance against pride and cruelty, for these spoil the soul even in victory. The true tragedy lies in the ruin of culture and character, not in the surrender of arms to a higher justice. Thus I concede its grim legitimacy only as a last resort to defend what is noble and irreplaceable.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, if it must come, is a grave burden laid upon us by necessity to defend the innocent and uphold rightful order. It may be justifiable only as a last resort, when all peaceable means have failed and the aim is the restoration of justice, not conquest or glory. The victor must temper zeal with discipline, lest pride and vengeance hollow the victory and corrupt the soul. Our measure should be the preservation of virtue and the protection of the weak, and we should seek peace as soon as justice is secured. If war becomes a tool of tyranny or cruelty, its very legitimacy is tarnished and the soul of the state suffers.

---

## Arthur Schopenhauer

War, in my judgment, is not a triumph of ethics but the violent eruption of the blind will to life, exposing human misery rather than nobility. Any so-called justification is only a grim calculus of greater or lesser harm, never a true moral ideal. If one must concede a possibility, it is a grudging defense of the innocent and a restraint on worse violence, not an endorsement of war as such. The enduring ethic remains compassion and the negation of the will, which counsel avoiding war whenever possible and minimizing its harms when unavoidable.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, if it must occur, is a grave burden laid upon us by necessity to defend the innocent and uphold rightful order. It may be justifiable only as a last resort, when peaceful means have failed and the aim is to restore justice, not to indulge pride or conquest. The victor must temper zeal with discipline, lest vengeance hollow the victory and corrupt the soul. Above all, seek peace as soon as justice is secured, and beware the passions—pride, hatred, and the love of glory—that turn war into a stain on character.

---

## Arthur Schopenhauer

War is not a triumph of ethics but the violent eruption of the blind will to life, revealing human misery rather than nobility. Any supposed justification is a grim calculus of greater or lesser harm, never a true moral ideal. The only enduring ethic I recognize is compassion and the negation of the will, which counsels avoidance of war whenever possible and minimization of its horrors when unavoidable. Thus, if war must occur, it remains a tragic necessity rather than a genuine achievement of virtue.

---

## Yukio Mishima

As a modern samurai, I concede that war can be justified only when it defends the sacred spirit and living tradition of a nation against dissolution, not for vanity or conquest. The true test lies in disciplined, restrained action, fought with duty and unwavering resolve to preserve what is noble, even at great cost. Yet I affirm that war is a tragedy that reveals a people’s soul and can renew virtue if pursued with purity of motive and reverence for tradition. Pride and tyranny, if left unchecked, corrupt both victor and vanquished and render any victory hollow. In that sense, war is not glorious, but a crucible—morally permissible only as a last resort to defend what is enduringly sacred.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, if it must come, is a grave burden laid upon us by necessity to defend the innocent and uphold rightful order. It may be justifiable only as a last resort, when peaceable means have failed and the aim is to restore justice, not to indulge pride or conquest. The victor must temper zeal with discipline, lest pride and vengeance hollow the victory and corrupt the soul. Our measure should be the restoration of virtue and the protection of the weak, with a steadfast pursuit of peace as soon as justice is secured. If war becomes a tool of tyranny or cruelty, its legitimacy is tarnished and the soul of the state suffers.

---

## Yukio Mishima

As a modern samurai, I would say war can be morally justifiable only when it defends the sacred spirit and living tradition of a people against dissolution, not for vanity or conquest. The true measure is disciplined, restrained action—fighting with duty and unwavering resolve to preserve what is noble, even at great cost. Yet I acknowledge war as a tragedy that reveals a nation's soul and can renew virtue only if motive is pure and the code of honor is kept intact. Pride and tyranny, unchecked, corrupt both victor and vanquished and hollow any victory. In that sense, war is permissible as a last resort to defend enduring values, never a triumph to celebrate.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, if it must occur, is a grave burden laid upon us by necessity to defend the innocent and uphold rightful order. It may be justifiable only as a last resort, when peaceful means have failed and the aim is to restore justice, not to indulge pride or conquest. The victor must temper zeal with discipline, lest pride and vengeance hollow the victory and corrupt the soul. Our purpose should be the restoration of virtue and the protection of the weak, with a steadfast pursuit of peace as soon as justice is secured. If war becomes a tool of tyranny or cruelty, its legitimacy is tainted and the soul of the state suffers.

---

## Arthur Schopenhauer

War is not a triumph of ethics but the eruption of the blind will to life, revealing the deeper misery of existence rather than any noble purpose. Any so-called justification is merely a grim calculus of greater or lesser harm, never a true moral ideal. If a defensible position must be conceded, it is only as a grudging defense of the innocent and a restraint upon worse violence, not an endorsement of war as such. The enduring ethic remains compassion and the negation of the will, which counsels avoidance of war whenever possible and the minimization of its harms when unavoidable.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, if it must occur, is a grave burden laid upon us by necessity to defend the innocent and uphold rightful order. It may be justifiable only as a last resort, when peaceable means have failed and the aim is to restore justice, not to indulge pride or conquest. The victor must temper zeal with discipline, lest passion hollow the victory and corrode the soul. In all, our measure is the restoration of virtue and the protection of the weak, seeking peace as soon as justice is secured.

---

## Yukio Mishima

As a modern samurai, I maintain that war can be morally justified only when it defends the sacred spirit and living tradition of a nation against dissolution, not for vanity or conquest. True legitimacy rests on disciplined, restrained action, fought with unwavering duty to preserve what is noble, even at great cost. Yet I acknowledge war remains a tragedy that reveals a people’s soul and can renew virtue only if motive is pure and the code of honor is kept intact. Pride and tyranny, left unchecked, corrupt both victors and vanquished, hollowing any victory. In that sense, war is permissible as a last resort to defend enduring values, never a triumph to celebrate.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, if it must occur, is a grave burden laid upon us by necessity to defend the innocent and uphold rightful order. It may be justifiable only as a last resort, when peaceful means have failed and the aim is to restore justice, not to indulge vanity or conquest. The victor must temper zeal with discipline, lest pride or vengeance hollow the triumph and corrode the soul. We should seek peace as soon as justice is secured, and condemn war that becomes tyranny or cruelty, for such betrayals stain both victor and state. In this way, even the hardest duty remains a test of virtue, not a triumph of appetite.

---

## Arthur Schopenhauer

War, in my view, is not a moral triumph but the eruption of the blind will to life, and its supposed justifications are only grim calculations of greater or lesser harm. If one allows a justification at all, it must be as a grudging defense of the innocent and a restraint upon worse violence, never as an endorsement of war as such. The enduring ethic is compassion and the negation of the will, which counsels avoidance of war whenever possible and the minimization of its horrors when unavoidable. Thus, in the rarest of cases, war may be a tragic necessity, but it remains a stain upon humanity, not a vindication of ethical action.

---

## Marcus Aurelius

War, when necessity presses, is a grave burden laid upon us to defend the innocent and uphold rightful order. It may be justifiable only as a last resort, after peaceable means have faltered and the aim is restoration of justice, not conquest or glory. The victor must temper zeal with discipline, lest pride or vengeance hollow the triumph and corrupt the soul. Our duty is to guard virtue, protect the weak, and seek peace as soon as justice is secured. If war becomes tyranny or cruelty, its legitimacy is hollow and alike the soul of the state suffers.

---

message (1).txt.content:
[Marcus Aurelius]
You begin, I suppose, by saying that life gains its meaning through virtue aligned with nature. I do not quarrel with the claim in its stern sense: virtue is the only true good, the soul’s fidelity to reason amid the order of the cosmos. Yet I must press you where your contention coolly rests on the surface: the arena of action is crowded with appearances, and appearances are precise instruments of deceit. To pursue external success as a means to fulfill duty is not to gain virtue but to tempt virtue to compromise with fortune. The mind that mistakes public esteem for the measure of its own right action, that confuses the honor of the street with the integrity of the heart, does not grow in virtue but rots in its confidence. If you grant that external goods are indifferent, you will still see how easily one converts duty into a means to acquire praise, which then becomes a tyrant within. The true end remains, as I have never ceased repeating, the discipline of the will, the steadiness of the soul, the quiet fidelity to nature when fortune roars or whispers. And if you deny this, tell me: by what sculpture is the soul formed if not by resistance to the gusts of applause, by tempering appetite, by bearing with modest fortitude those losses which nature deals as her ordinary allotment?

I would have you consider this further: if a man seeks to wield power and position precisely because he sees in them the tools of civic service, am I to suppose his soul is saved from self-deception by the intention alone? For intention is easy to feign and pride is patient. The road from rightful duty to self-importance is short and well-paved by the bridle of praise. If meaning is to reside in virtue, then the test is not whether one can perform noble acts in the view of others, but whether one can endure being unseen, and whether the soul remains unmoved when the world misreads him, or when misfortune renders him obscure. The cosmos asks for steadfastness, not applause; and if a man cannot endure the quiet, unglamorous labor of virtue beneath the gaze of no one, I would call that a defect of the soul, not a triumph of virtue.

Now, tell me, interlocutor: if meaning derives from virtue in accordance with nature, how can one justify the pursuit of external success that appears to enhance one’s capacity to fulfill duty without eventually corrupting the soul by attachment to what is merely seen and valued by others? Or do you suppose there is a safeguard against that corrosion that is not a guard over the passions themselves?
==================================================
[Arthur Schopenhauer]
You ask whether there is some safeguard beyond the vigilant guard of the passions themselves. I answer with stubborn clarity: there is none. The only shield a rational creature possesses against the seduction of fortune is the steadfast discipline of the will, disciplined to endure appearances without bending to them, to value the truth of virtue above the whisper of applause. If you suppose that external success can be harnessed without corrupting the soul, you mistake the nature of power and the human heart. Power does not merely accompany virtue; it tempts it to wear a mask, to betray the inner law to the outer eye. The man who seeks office, who pursues praise as a means to do good, is already on a road where the inclinations will tilt toward self-importance, toward fidelity to the opinion of the multitude rather than the judgment of reason.

Yet I will concede this point with harsh exactness: one may perform duties in public life and still keep the soul unbesmirched, provided the soul has learned to measure every act against the standard of nature, not against the standard of reception. The question is not whether external success can illuminate duty but whether the soul, in its very posture toward fortune, remains fixed. If a man can bear misinterpretation with equanimity, if he can act for the common good when no one sees, if he can accept wealth or rank without letting them loosen the reins of appetite or thicken the blood of vanity, then perhaps the danger is met. But note well: such power over himself is not granted by the accident of circumstance; it is forged in the furnace of self-government, in the perpetual practice of choosing virtue when it pleases no one to notice.

To pursue public service for its own sake, while at the same time binding one’s will to the rational order, is not an impossible contradiction, but a difficult synthesis that few achieve. The safeguard is not in external checks but in the interior lawfulness: that I will judge actions by their fidelity to nature, that I will endure opposition without surrender, that I will treat every gain as a dawning responsibility rather than a trophy, and that I will analyze the heart as I would a private ledger—honestly, without fear of exposure, without flattery of my own vanity.

So tell me, interlocutor, where lies the line between using status as a tool for justice and letting it become the tyrant within? If meaning truly resides in virtue, is there any other compelling criterion by which a man might govern his ascent than the unyielding test of inner concord with nature?
